text: holding back a groan, she followed him into the valley . “At least we’ll be going downhill for the next minute or so,” he offered . “Oh? I hadn’t noticed. Are you going to tell me next that we have a big hill to climb ?” Ronin lifted his chin. “ We do .” “I’d kick you, if it would hurt you more than me .” “We’ve already established that I have feelings. Those can be hurt , too .” “But my foot would be hurting more. Besides, you know I’d kiss and make up later .” “Wouldn’t it be more enjoyable for both of us to skip the kick and move directly to the kiss , then ?” “Take my frustrations out on you with sex?” She grinned, the thought sending a rush of excitement through her despite everything. “It might work .” Ronin stopped and turned to face her. His eyes roamed over her; he was considering it ! “You’ve got more walking to do, before you earn that privilege,” he said, displaying a grin of his own . “Oh, really? Well, then, I think I might be too worn out for it by then, anyway.” She walked past him . “I thought you already are, with the way you’ve been talking .” “ Smart ass .” “My ass is, at best, of average intelligence .” Lara shook her head and smirked. His footsteps crunched behind her as he caught up . Soon, she lost count of the hills. The irregular cycle of climbing and descending — short of breath and muscles aflame going up, limbs heavy and rubbery going down — took a new toll on her. She pushed on nonetheless, driven only by pride and stubbornness, but she couldn’t deny her relief and gratitude when he finally took her in his arms and carried her . Once again, his pace increased . Large rock formations came into view as afternoon faded into evening. Lara stared at them in fascination as they finally drew near; the stones were multi-layered and strangely smooth. Her focus was so intent that she jumped at the first boom of thunder . Ronin halted, twisting to look back . “Oh, shit.” Lara’s eyes widened . The sky, normally blanketed with a persistent, yellowish haze, was black with storm clouds in the east. Everything between the clouds and the ground was obscured by dust. It stretched as far as she could see from north to south; a massive, moving wall of dirt . Ronin spun, head turning as though searching, but Lara couldn’t take her eyes off the dust storm. Though it was distant, she knew it was coming toward them. And it was moving fast . Back in Cheyenne, she’d weathered many such storms. People hunkered down in their shacks, tied down doors and shutters, and hoped everything would hold together. If you were well enough to repair your home the next day, it was a victory . She’d never been caught by one in the open . “Hold on , Lara .” She clutched Ronin as he ran . Chapter Twenty - Four E ven before breaking into a sprint, Ronin knew there’d be no outrunning the storm. Their only hope was shelter. His processors worked in overdrive, poring over stored data in the search for a shelter somewhere nearby — a building, or a cave, or even a partially collapsed wall to hide behind . Lara clung to him, her face buried against his shoulder. The wind was strong at his back, pushing him forward, whipping her hair into his optics. If they’d had the chance to resupply before leaving Cheyenne, he might have located some sort of air filtration system for her. That would have diminished at least one of the dangers they were about to face . Lightning flashed, followed two and a half seconds later by a crack of thunder. Lara lifted her head and wadded his coat in her fists. “ Ronin !” “We’re going to be fine,” he said, amplifying his vocal synthesizer so she could hear him over the roaring wind . He ran up a hill into a copse of trees, several which were still living. If there were no alternatives, the ancient boughs would help break up the wind a little, but they wouldn’t provide adequate shelter for Lara. He should have kept their course closer to the old road. At least then, he might have located a culvert or something of the sort they could shelter within . Finally emerging from the trees, he stopped on the crest of the hill. The land before him leveled out into a wide, open expanse, littered with brush, weeds, brown grass, and dirt, only rising into another hill in the distance. Ronin swept his optics across the area, catching a contrast in color several hundred yards out. Buildings . Ronin worked his way down to the open ground. Several hundred pounds of gear — their clothing, ammunition, tools, food, water, and two hauls’ worth of scrap — in addition to Lara in his arms had his compensators and actuators operating well above their normal levels of strain. If she was jostled by his speed, she made no complaint . The only standing structure was a house, and as they neared, it was increasingly clear that time had not been kind to it. Paint was peeling off the wooden siding, and the siding itself had been so exposed to the elements that it, too, was peeling away from the underlying framework. The entire house leaned precariously to one side, three degrees off-center. The roof sagged, all the windows were shattered, and the front door hung on a single hinge . He slowed when they were within fifty feet of the building. It couldn’t be trusted to hold up to the punishment the dust storm was about to unleash. All the visual evidence suggested the house should have collapsed on itself years ago; it was a
